input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system is the first vertical axis of a 6-axis robot used in manufacturing for machining tasks, characterized by precise motion control requirements within complex production environments.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) employs SysML for system modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, technical), with methodologies RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior is described via MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: MBSE utilizes SysML for structural modeling (including component interfaces and composition via Internal Block Diagrams), MontiArc Automaton for behavioral modeling using state machines, and MathWorks Simulink for analytical simulations. Data sources originate from physical systems through automated one-way data flows as defined in digital shadow reference models, with data management involving aggregation and abstraction of contextual traces for specific purposes. Models are structured across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) and integrated across heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives using ADLs such as AADL, ACME, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical system includes a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. Actuation mechanisms are managed through this industrial controller, which provides precise motion control capabilities for the robotic system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox, enabling precise measurement of rotational position and velocity for feedback control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data including input current (I_M) and output angle is streamed from physical systems to the digital shadow via MQTT protocol. Data traces are aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes; however, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and data integration mechanisms beyond aggregation are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for transmitting time series sensor data (e.g., input current I_M and output angle) from physical to digital components. Network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability and redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a step-by-step methodology: 1) Problem identification to gather all relevant requirements; 2) Asset and model analysis to understand the physical system's structure and behavior; 3) Use or creation of calculations or models to simulate system behavior; 4) Data identification to determine necessary data streams for the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Industry standards such as SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are adopted for system modeling, enabling component & connector-based descriptions across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) and integration of heterogeneous stakeholders and tools.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the first vertical axis of a 6-axis robot used in manufacturing, which matches the source. However, the source does not explicitly mention 'complex production environments', though it is implied by the context of machining tasks. The extracted text is specific and correct, but could be more detailed about the robot model (MABI MAX 100). Score 4 (Good).

The source document does not contain information about the services provided by the digital twin (or shadow) as described in the characteristic description. The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is correct. Therefore, the score is 5.

The extracted text is a very close paraphrase of the source, containing all key details about MBSE, ADLs, and behavior description methods. It is highly specific, detailed, and strongly grounded in the provided sources.
